pattern: Collect only the handful of cells that actually sit on either diagonal, then apply a simple trial-division primality test (checking divisors up to the square root) to keep the whole scan close to linear in the matrix size.
hints: You only ever need to look at up to two cells per row: the main-diagonal cell and the anti-diagonal cell -- everything else in the matrix is irrelevant.
Write a small standalone primality check that tests divisibility only up to the square root of the number, handling values below 2 as never prime.
Walk the diagonal cells once, run each one through the primality check, and keep the largest one that passes; if none pass, the answer is 0.

edge_inputs.description: largest diagonal value happens to be prime
a middle diagonal value is the largest prime, not a corner
1x1 matrix whose only cell is prime
1x1 matrix whose only cell is not prime
the largest diagonal value is not prime, but a smaller diagonal value is -- must not just check the maximum
no diagonal value is prime at all, must return 0
same value repeated on both diagonals at the center cell
a large prime near the top of the value range on the diagonal

stress.description: random square matrices up to the maximum side length with values across the full allowed range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 300))
    nums = [[rng.randint(1, 4000000) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
